titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Gordon Parks, Study Sets of Photographs
titlestmt.author: by Matthew Daniel Mason

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.physdesc: 13 boxes
did.abstract: Collection of eleven "study sets" that represent selections from series created by Parks, chiefly 1942-1976. Photographic prints in the sets include images reproduced by the Gordon Parks Foundation, 2012-2021, as well as examples of prints created by Parks during his professional life, 1942-2006.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from the Gordon Parks Foundation on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund and the Frederick W. and Carrie S. Beinecke Fund for Western Americana, 2021.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into 11 series: I. Study Set 1. Washington D.C., 1942. II. Study Set 2. Back to Fort Scott, 1950. III. Study Set 3. Invisible Man, 1952. IV. Study Set 4. Crime, 1957. V. Study Set 5. Segregation in the South, 1956. VI. Study Set 6. Mamie and Kenneth Clark, 1947. VII. Study Set 7, Harlem, 1967. VIII. Study Set 8, Civil Rights, 1963. IX. Study Set 9, The Flávio Story, 1961, 1976. X. Study Set 10, Gordon Parks X Muhammad Ali, 1966, 1970. XI. Study Set 11, Harlem Gang Leader, 1948.

bioghist.head: Gordon Parks (1912-2006)
bioghist.p: Gordon Parks was an African American photographer, musician, writer, and film director. He rose to prominence in documentary photojournalism during the 1940s through 1970s, especially surrounding issues of civil rights, poverty and African Americans as well as fashion photography.

bioghist.head: Gordon Parks Foundation
bioghist.p: The Gordon Parks Foundation based in Pleasantville, New York, permanently preserves the work of Gordon Parks, makes it available to the public through exhibitions, books, and electronic media and supports artistic and educational activities that advance what Parks described as "the common search for a better life and a better world."

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Gordon Parks, Study Sets of Photographs. James Weldon Johnson Memorial Collection in the Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit. These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Beinecke Manuscript Unit Processing Manual.
Each folder in the collection contains a single photographic print. Discrete study sets in the collection may be housed in multiple boxes that may not have sequential numbers.

The Gordon Parks Foundation was the immediate source for prints in this collection. The organization authenticates prints created by Parks during his life and creates limited edition reproductions as black-and-white gelatin silver prints and color inkjet prints from his negatives as well as assigning an number for each image (usually expressed as ##.###) and an unique "GP" number for each discrete print.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Collection of eleven "study sets" that represent selections from series created by Parks, chiefly 1942-1976. Photographic prints in the sets include images reproduced by the Gordon Parks Foundation, 2012-2021, as well as examples of prints created by Parks during his professional life, 1942-2006.

The study sets from the 1940s include Washington D.C., a series created for the United States Farm Security Administration that documents the life of Ella Watson, an African American custodial worker for the federal government in 1942; Mamie and Kenneth Clark, created for Ebony magazine on the married psychologist team of Mamie Phipps Clark and Kenneth Bancroft Clark in 1947, whose research on the effects of segregation on Black children influenced the decision in Brown v. Board of Education (1954); and Harlem Gang Leader, Park's first project for Life magazine in 1948, which documented Leonard "Red" Jackson, a young black man and leader of the Midtowners gang.

The study sets from the 1950s include Back to Fort Scott, an ultimately unpublished series for Life where Parks revisited his childhood hometown and documented the lives of his junior high school classmates; Invisible Man, a series for Life in 1952 based on the novel of the same name published that year by Ralph Ellison where Parks sought to visually draw passages from the novel by creating images of a friend on the streets of Harlem; Segregation in the South, a series for Life in 1956 that chiefly documented the lives of an African American sharecropping family in Alabama as well as "separate but equal" facilities in public transportation between Brimingham, Alabama, and Nashville, Tennessee; and Crime, a series that supported a recurring set of articles in Life magazine in 1957 on crime in the United States.

The study sets from the 1960s include Civil Rights, photographs for Life in 1966 that document the growing Civil Rights Movement as well as the Black Muslim movement and the leadership of the Nation of Islam, including Malcolm X; and Harlem, a series for Life in 1967 that focused on the Fontenelle family, a West Indian immigrant family struggling with poverty, alcoholism, and domestic violence.

Study sets that straddle the 1960s and 1970s include The Flávio Story, a series on poverty in the favelas of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, in 1961 and 1976, which documents Brazilian boy Flávio da Silva and his family for Life, and Gordon Parks X Muhammad Ali, a series of portraits of the heavyweight boxer for Life, 1966 and 1970.

Prints in the collection made by Parks during his lifetime were bequeathed to The Gordon Parks Foundation. Prints reproduced and distributed by the Gordon Parks Foundation include gelatin silver prints by Griffin Editions and inkjet prints by Adamson Editions.

did.physdesc: (2 boxes)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs from Washington D.C. , a series created for the Farm Security Administration that documents the life of Ella Watson (1883-1980), an African American custodial worker for the federal government in 1942, as well as her foster daughter and lodger Lauretta Burgess (born approximately 1926) and other relatives and friends. Watson was born Ella Hearns in Washington, D.C. and in 1900 married George Warren Watson (1882-1927) and they had two children, Alice Watson and Eugene Watson. After leaving school in 1898, she worked as a laundress for the Frazee Laundry and then she labored for the United States government chiefly as a custodial worker from approximately 1919 to 1944. Watson was also a member of St. Martin's Spiritual Church (also known as the Verbycke Spiritual Church after its pastor Vondell Gassaway). When she died at the Prince George's Hospital convalescent home, she had six grandchildren, 22 great grandchildren, and one great grandchild.

did.physdesc: (2 boxes)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs from the Invisible Man series created in 1952 based on the eponymous novel by Ralph Ellison (1913-1994) where Park sought to visually draw passages from the work by creating portraits of an African American man on the streets of Harlem. The subject was John Henry Bates (1909-1975), a cabinet maker at whose farm in Waitsfield, Vermont, Ellison began writing Invisible Man during the summer of 1945.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under three headings: 8 x 10 Inches, 11 x 14 Inches, and 16 x 20 Inches.

did.unittitle: Study Set 5. Segregation in the South
did.physdesc: (2 boxes)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs from "Segregation in the South", a series for Life in 1956 that chiefly documented the lives of an African American sharecropping family in Alabama as well as "separate but equal" facilities in public transportation between Brimingham, Alabama, and Nashville, Tennessee.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: 16 x 20 Inches and 24 x 24 inches.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged sequentially by Gordon Parks Prefix Numbers.

J. (Erly Joseph) Thornton (1908-1973) and his wife Etta Ruth McCalum Thornton (1914-1998) as well as children E. J. (Erly Joseph) Thornton (1946-2020), Lillian Thornton Lisbon (1943-2010), and Ruth E. Thornton Hawkins (1939-2017). The eldere E. J. Thornton was professor of poultry husbandry at Tennessee Agricultural and Industrial State College (later Tennessee State University) and a son of Albert Thornton (1874-1964) and Josie Maye Thornton (1886-1990).

Ondria Wilson Thornton (born approximately 1917) was married to Revert Thornton (born 1901) until they divorced in 1956. Ondria Tanner was a daughter of Virgie Lee Thornton Tanner (1932-1991) and Henry W. Tanner (1922-1993).

did.unittitle: Study Set 6. Mamie and Kenneth Clark
did.physdesc: (1 box)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs from a study set, Mamie and Kenneth Clark , created for the article "Problem Kids" published in Ebony in July 1947 about the psychologist team of Mamie Phipps Clark (1917-1983) and Kenneth Bancroft Clark (1914-2005) in 1947, whose research on the effects of segregation on Black children influenced the decision in Brown v. Board of Education (1954). All the prints in this series measure 20 x 16 inches (50.9 x 40.7 cm).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs for a series for Life in 1961 and 1976 that documents poverty in the favelas of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, with a focus on Brazilian boy Flávio da Silva (born approximately 1949) and his family.

did.physdesc: (2 boxes)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document Leonard "Red" Jackson (born 1932), a young African American man and leader of the Midtowners gang at Harlem in 1948. There series was for the first project for Life by Gordon Parks.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under three headings: 8 x 10 Inches, 11 x 14 Inches, and 20 x 24 Inches.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged sequentially by Gordon Parks Prefix Numbers.
